content:
Singular value decomposition was developed independently in the 19th century by **Beltrami** and **Jordan** for bilinear forms, and later unified in numerical linear algebra.

**Eckart** and **Young** (1936) proved that truncated SVD gives the best low-rank matrix approximation—providing theoretical justification for compression and factor models decades before modern ML.

**Golub** and **Kahan** (1965) introduced practical bidiagonalization algorithms that made SVD feasible on computers; Golub–Van Loan style routines power libraries like LAPACK and NumPy today.

In ML, SVD became central to latent semantic indexing (1990s), collaborative filtering and PCA at scale, and remains the conceptual backbone behind many embedding and matrix-factorization methods even as nonlinear deep models grew.

timeline.label: Theoretical origins
timeline.description: Mathematicians characterize matrix factorizations via singular values of linear operators.

timeline.label: Eckart–Young theorem
timeline.description: Optimal low-rank approximation property motivates truncation as principled compression.

timeline.label: Numerical algorithms
timeline.description: Bidiagonalization and QR-style sweeps enable stable SVD on large matrices.

timeline.label: ML factor models
timeline.description: LSI, recommender systems and PCA pipelines adopt truncated SVD as a default tool.
